title: Error Handling
summary: Catch and recover from failures with try/catch/finally, throw your own errors, and handle rejected promises.

blocks.md: Things go wrong — bad input, a failed network request, a missing file. **Error handling** lets your program respond gracefully instead of crashing. The core tool is `try...catch`.

blocks.caption: try / catch / finally

blocks.md: Code in `try` runs normally; if anything **throws**, control jumps to `catch` with the error object. `finally` runs either way — perfect for cleanup.

blocks.caption: throw when something is invalid

blocks.title: try/catch doesn't catch async callbacks
blocks.md: A `try/catch` only catches errors thrown **synchronously** in its block. For promises, either use `.catch()`, or `await` inside a `try/catch` block (async/await makes rejected promises throwable). A plain `setTimeout` callback that throws won't be caught by an outer try/catch.

blocks.caption: with async/await

blocks.question: When does the `finally` block run?
options: Only when no error was thrown
Only when an error was thrown
Always — whether or not an error was thrown
Never, unless you call it manually
blocks.explanation: finally always runs after try/catch complete, regardless of whether an error occurred — which makes it ideal for cleanup like closing a file or hiding a spinner.

keyTakeaways: `try` runs code; if it throws, `catch (err)` handles the error; `finally` always runs.
Use `throw new Error('message')` to signal an invalid situation.
The caught error object has a `.message` (and `.name`, `.stack`).
Plain try/catch only catches synchronous throws — use `.catch()` or `await` in try/catch for promises.

exercise.prompt: Write `safeParse(text)` that returns the parsed JSON, or `null` if parsing fails (instead of throwing).
exercise.hint: Wrap JSON.parse in try/catch; return the result in try, return null in catch.
